explanationHtml:
Step 1: Recall the discriminant rule.
A quadratic \(ax^2+bx+c=0\) has exactly one distinct real solution when its discriminant \(\Delta=b^2-4ac=0\).

Step 2: Compute \(\Delta\) for each choice.

  • Choice A — ‘\(x^2-16=0\)’: \(a=1,b=0,c=-16\). \(\Delta=0-4(1)(-16)=64>0\) → two real solutions ✗
  • Choice B — ‘\(x^2+16=0\)’: \(a=1,b=0,c=16\). \(\Delta=0-64=-64<0\) → no real solutions ✗
  • Choice C — ‘\(x^2-16x+56=0\)’: \(a=1,b=-16,c=56\). \(\Delta=256-224=32>0\) → two real solutions ✗
  • Choice D — ‘\(x^2-16x+64=0\)’: \(a=1,b=-16,c=64\). \(\Delta=256-256=0\) → exactly one real solution \((x=8)\) ✓

That matches choice D — D is correct.
